MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper includes a concrete baseline for concept-map generation and evaluates it on the new DIP corpus using multiple proposition-level metrics (strict/METEOR/ROUGE), so comparisons are performed under consistent conditions. However, only a simple pipeline baseline is presented, and more directly comparable prior work (e.g., learned concept-map summarization systems like Concept-Map-Based MDS [2017]) or modern salience-learning baselines are not empirically compared, which limits claims about improvements. The authors do analyze failure modes (content selection and relation extraction bottlenecks) and discuss limitations, but they stop short of demonstrating superiority to or parity with the most relevant competing approaches. Overall the comparisons are fair but incomplete with respect to key recent baselines. | B(3): The paper performs some comparisons (a baseline system and a pilot on TAC2008) and evaluates with multiple metrics on the same data, so comparisons are generally fair. However, the main baseline is rather simple and the work does not empirically compare against stronger, directly relevant prior methods for concept-map-based multi-document summarization (e.g. concept-coreference or learned salience models such as in the retrieved related work [1]), so key baselines are missing. The authors acknowledge limitations (content selection and relation extraction) and analyze failure modes, but they do not demonstrate how existing competing approaches perform on their new task or dataset. Overall the comparisons are useful but incomplete relative to the most relevant prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes a concrete baseline for concept-map generation and evaluates it on the new DIP corpus using multiple proposition-level metrics (strict/METEOR/ROUGE), so comparisons are performed under consistent conditions. However, only a simple pipeline baseline is presented, and more directly comparable prior work (e.g., learned concept-map summarization systems like Concept-Map-Based MDS [2017]) or modern salience-learning baselines are not empirically compared, which limits claims about improvements. The authors do analyze failure modes (content selection and relation extraction bottlenecks) and discuss limitations, but they stop short of demonstrating superiority to or parity with the most relevant competing approaches. Overall the comparisons are fair but incomplete with respect to key recent baselines.

However, the main baseline is rather simple and the work does not empirically compare against stronger, directly relevant prior methods for concept-map-based multi-document summarization (e.g. concept-coreference or learned salience models such as in the retrieved related work [1]), so key baselines are missing. The authors acknowledge limitations (content selection and relation extraction) and analyze failure modes, but they do not demonstrate how existing competing approaches perform on their new task or dataset. Overall the comparisons are useful but incomplete relative to the most relevant prior work.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—structuring and summarizing large heterogeneous web document clusters—and provides concrete artifacts (the DIP corpus, a baseline system, and released code) that materially lower the barrier for follow-up work. The novel low-context importance annotation protocol and the end-to-end pipeline (Open IE + confidence filtering + crowdsourced ranking + expert revision) are likely to influence how researchers collect and validate summary units at scale, even though relation extraction and content-selection remain clear bottlenecks in the provided baseline. Given the corpus, evaluation protocol, and evidence that the annotation scheme yields reliable importance signals, this work opens useful new directions and is likely to be built upon by the community. | B(4): This work addresses an important practical gap—summarizing large, heterogeneous web document clusters with structured concept maps—and supplies a valuable new corpus (30-topic DIP) plus code and baseline that the community can build on. The introduced low-context importance annotation combines Likert and pairwise sampling with TrueSkill and appears to yield reliable importance estimates, which could change how large-scale importance annotation and summary evaluation are done. While relation extraction and content selection remain challenging (baseline captures only ~17% of gold concepts and strict proposition matching is low), the dataset, protocol, and demonstrated evaluation utility are likely to open substantial new research directions across multidocument summarization and structured-summary evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—structuring and summarizing large heterogeneous web document clusters—and provides concrete artifacts (the DIP corpus, a baseline system, and released code) that materially lower the barrier for follow-up work. The novel low-context importance annotation protocol and the end-to-end pipeline (Open IE + confidence filtering + crowdsourced ranking + expert revision) are likely to influence how researchers collect and validate summary units at scale, even though relation extraction and content-selection remain clear bottlenecks in the provided baseline. Given the corpus, evaluation protocol, and evidence that the annotation scheme yields reliable importance signals, this work opens useful new directions and is likely to be built upon by the community.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses an important practical gap—summarizing large, heterogeneous web document clusters with structured concept maps—and supplies a valuable new corpus (30-topic DIP) plus code and baseline that the community can build on. The introduced low-context importance annotation combines Likert and pairwise sampling with TrueSkill and appears to yield reliable importance estimates, which could change how large-scale importance annotation and summary evaluation are done. While relation extraction and content selection remain challenging (baseline captures only ~17% of gold concepts and strict proposition matching is low), the dataset, protocol, and demonstrated evaluation utility are likely to open substantial new research directions across multidocument summarization and structured-summary evaluation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper delivers a substantial new corpus, a detailed crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation), and a reproducible pipeline with code—supporting many of its claims about annotation reliability and utility. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting), and the experimental analysis lacks important sensitivity/ablation studies (e.g., effect of OpenIE errors, TrueSkill vs alternatives, sampling variance, and pipeline component ablations) that would justify pipeline choices. Related work coverage is adequate and the authors discuss limitations (content selection and relation extraction bottlenecks), but more rigorous robustness analyses and statistical testing are needed to fully support the claims relative to prior concept-map MDS work (e.g., similar 2017 concept-map approaches). | B(4): The paper provides solid empirical support for its main claims: a new corpus, a novel low-context crowdsourcing protocol with reliability analyses, and a baseline pipeline with multiple automatic evaluation metrics (strict/METEOR/ROUGE) showing concrete failure modes (content selection, relation extraction). Related work coverage appears adequate given similar concept-map and salience estimation work (e.g. concept-map MDS approaches and VAE salience models), and limitations are explicitly discussed. Missing, however, are deeper ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g. effect of TrueSkill parameters or OpenIE confidence thresholds) and some reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported), which constitute minor gaps preventing a perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper delivers a substantial new corpus, a detailed crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation), and a reproducible pipeline with code—supporting many of its claims about annotation reliability and utility. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting), and the experimental analysis lacks important sensitivity/ablation studies (e.g., effect of OpenIE errors, TrueSkill vs alternatives, sampling variance, and pipeline component ablations) that would justify pipeline choices. Related work coverage is adequate and the authors discuss limitations (content selection and relation extraction bottlenecks), but more rigorous robustness analyses and statistical testing are needed to fully support the claims relative to prior concept-map MDS work (e.g., similar 2017 concept-map approaches).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical support for its main claims: a new corpus, a novel low-context crowdsourcing protocol with reliability analyses, and a baseline pipeline with multiple automatic evaluation metrics (strict/METEOR/ROUGE) showing concrete failure modes (content selection, relation extraction). Related work coverage appears adequate given similar concept-map and salience estimation work (e.g. concept-map MDS approaches and VAE salience models), and limitations are explicitly discussed. Missing, however, are deeper ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g. effect of TrueSkill parameters or OpenIE confidence thresholds) and some reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported), which constitute minor gaps preventing a perfect score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental evidence (crowdsourced importance annotations with reported agreement/correlations, several automatic evaluation metrics, and a released pipeline and code) supporting them. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. However, reproducibility and methodological rigor have minor shortcomings: hyperparameters are not reported, the strongest baseline is relatively simple and content-selection remains a major failure mode, and it is unclear whether statistical significance testing or sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to Open IE errors or annotator variability) were performed. Overall the conclusions are plausible and largely supported, but a few missing experimental details and stronger ablations would be needed for a technically flawless claim. | B(4): Overall the paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by a coherent experimental pipeline and empirical results (crowdsourcing reliability, utility for evaluation, and a baseline system showing clear failure modes). There are no mathematical proofs to check; the methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines, multiple evaluation metrics, and acknowledgement of limitations (content selection and relation extraction). Minor technical issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and statistical testing details are not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and some claims about significance. Given these are implementation/practical gaps rather than fundamental errors, I judge the work technically solid with only minor concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental evidence (crowdsourced importance annotations with reported agreement/correlations, several automatic evaluation metrics, and a released pipeline and code) supporting them. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. However, reproducibility and methodological rigor have minor shortcomings: hyperparameters are not reported, the strongest baseline is relatively simple and content-selection remains a major failure mode, and it is unclear whether statistical significance testing or sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to Open IE errors or annotator variability) were performed. Overall the conclusions are plausible and largely supported, but a few missing experimental details and stronger ablations would be needed for a technically flawless claim.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by a coherent experimental pipeline and empirical results (crowdsourcing reliability, utility for evaluation, and a baseline system showing clear failure modes). There are no mathematical proofs to check; the methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines, multiple evaluation metrics, and acknowledgement of limitations (content selection and relation extraction). Minor technical issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and statistical testing details are not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and some claims about significance. Given these are implementation/practical gaps rather than fundamental errors, I judge the work technically solid with only minor concerns.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP task (multi-document summarization / structured summarization) and introduces a new corpus, a novel crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation), and an end-to-end pipeline with a released baseline and code — all well within the scope and interests of a top NLP/ML venue. Methods and evaluations use community-expected standards (strict and METEOR proposition matching, ROUGE, comparisons to prior datasets like TAC2008), and the audience (summarization, information extraction, annotation methodology) is appropriate. My main reservations for a perfect score are reproducibility details: hyperparameters are not reported and relation-extraction remains a clear bottleneck, which limits immediate reproducibility and comparative follow-up work. Overall the paper is a strong fit with only minor scope/reproducibility concerns. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it releases a large, carefully constructed corpus (DIP topics with human-authored concept maps), introduces a novel and practical crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill) and provides a reproducible pipeline, baseline, and evaluation protocol. The problem formulation—multidocument summarization framed as concept-map generation—is appropriate and timely for the community, and the methods and evaluation (strict and METEOR-based proposition matching, ROUGE-2, and reliability analyses) meet venue standards. Positioning and related-work coverage are adequate, and the stated limitations (content selection and relation-extraction bottlenecks) are candid and useful for guiding follow-up research.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (multi-document summarization / structured summarization) and introduces a new corpus, a novel crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation), and an end-to-end pipeline with a released baseline and code — all well within the scope and interests of a top NLP/ML venue. Methods and evaluations use community-expected standards (strict and METEOR proposition matching, ROUGE, comparisons to prior datasets like TAC2008), and the audience (summarization, information extraction, annotation methodology) is appropriate. My main reservations for a perfect score are reproducibility details: hyperparameters are not reported and relation-extraction remains a clear bottleneck, which limits immediate reproducibility and comparative follow-up work. Overall the paper is a strong fit with only minor scope/reproducibility concerns.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it releases a large, carefully constructed corpus (DIP topics with human-authored concept maps), introduces a novel and practical crowdsourcing protocol (low-context importance annotation with TrueSkill) and provides a reproducible pipeline, baseline, and evaluation protocol. The problem formulation—multidocument summarization framed as concept-map generation—is appropriate and timely for the community, and the methods and evaluation (strict and METEOR-based proposition matching, ROUGE-2, and reliability analyses) meet venue standards. Positioning and related-work coverage are adequate, and the stated limitations (content selection and relation-extraction bottlenecks) are candid and useful for guiding follow-up research.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, and pipeline/baseline) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and the novel crowdsourcing protocol is described with enough detail to understand the pipeline, while figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. However, some presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-focused readers (e.g., hyperparameters are not reported), and a few methodological details (e.g., exact Open IE confidence thresholds) could be more explicitly specified to fully support reimplementation. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (new corpus, low-context importance annotation, and an automatic pipeline), making the narrative easy to follow from motivation through experiments to conclusions. Notation and terminology appear consistent and the methodological steps (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced ranking, expert revision) are described in a logical order; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., some experimental details (hyperparameters) are omitted and a few sections on baseline design and failure modes could be expanded for clarity—but these do not impede understanding.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (new corpus, crowdsourcing scheme, and pipeline/baseline) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and the novel crowdsourcing protocol is described with enough detail to understand the pipeline, while figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. However, some presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-focused readers (e.g., hyperparameters are not reported), and a few methodological details (e.g., exact Open IE confidence thresholds) could be more explicitly specified to fully support reimplementation.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (new corpus, low-context importance annotation, and an automatic pipeline), making the narrative easy to follow from motivation through experiments to conclusions. Notation and terminology appear consistent and the methodological steps (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced ranking, expert revision) are described in a logical order; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., some experimental details (hyperparameters) are omitted and a few sections on baseline design and failure modes could be expanded for clarity—but these do not impede understanding.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides notable novelty by introducing a new, large corpus of human-authored summary concept maps for heterogeneous web document clusters and proposing the low-context importance annotation scheme for scalable crowdsourced salience estimation—contributions that go beyond typical sentence-level salience work. While concept-map summarization itself has prior art (e.g., 2017 concept-map-based MDS), the combination of large-cluster focus, a tailored crowdsourcing protocol (Likert + pairwise TrueSkill), and an end-to-end pipeline (Open IE + crowd ranking + expert revision) represents a clear, non-trivial advance rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing techniques. That said, core NLP components (Open IE, confidence filtering, importance ranking) are applied in expected ways, so the novelty is primarily in task design, dataset, and annotation methodology rather than in fundamentally new algorithms. | B(4): The paper presents notable novelty in task design and resources: the low-context importance annotation crowdsourcing scheme and a released corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries for large, heterogeneous web document clusters are new and address a clear gap in scalable importance estimation and evaluation. While concept-map-based summarization has prior work (e.g., 2017 concept-map summarization approaches) the authors go beyond it by combining OpenIE-driven proposition extraction, a novel low-context annotation protocol with TrueSkill-based aggregation, and an end-to-end pipeline with expert revision—an integration that materially advances practical methodology. Thus the contribution is more than incremental, though not a radical departure from existing concept-map summarization literature, so I rate it as notably novel rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides notable novelty by introducing a new, large corpus of human-authored summary concept maps for heterogeneous web document clusters and proposing the low-context importance annotation scheme for scalable crowdsourced salience estimation—contributions that go beyond typical sentence-level salience work. While concept-map summarization itself has prior art (e.g., 2017 concept-map-based MDS), the combination of large-cluster focus, a tailored crowdsourcing protocol (Likert + pairwise TrueSkill), and an end-to-end pipeline (Open IE + crowd ranking + expert revision) represents a clear, non-trivial advance rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing techniques. That said, core NLP components (Open IE, confidence filtering, importance ranking) are applied in expected ways, so the novelty is primarily in task design, dataset, and annotation methodology rather than in fundamentally new algorithms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents notable novelty in task design and resources: the low-context importance annotation crowdsourcing scheme and a released corpus of human-authored concept-map summaries for large, heterogeneous web document clusters are new and address a clear gap in scalable importance estimation and evaluation. While concept-map-based summarization has prior work (e.g., 2017 concept-map summarization approaches) the authors go beyond it by combining OpenIE-driven proposition extraction, a novel low-context annotation protocol with TrueSkill-based aggregation, and an end-to-end pipeline with expert revision—an integration that materially advances practical methodology. Thus the contribution is more than incremental, though not a radical departure from existing concept-map summarization literature, so I rate it as notably novel rather than groundbreaking.

main_claims: They present a newly created corpus of human-authored summary concept maps for large, heterogeneous web document clusters on educational topics.

They introduce a novel crowdsourcing scheme, called low-context importance annotation (combining Likert scoring and pairwise comparisons with TrueSkill), to reliably determine important propositions in large document collections.

They provide an automatic pipeline (Open IE extraction, confidence filtering, crowdsourced importance ranking, expert revision) to produce high-quality concept-map summaries and release a baseline system and evaluation protocol.

They demonstrate that the crowdsourcing approach yields reliable importance annotations (high agreement and correlations) and that their annotations are useful for summary evaluation (comparable to pyramid/ROUGE-based evaluation).
_summary.methodology: They extract propositions from relevant sentences using Open IE, filter by extraction confidence, crowdsource importance via Likert scores and sampled pairwise comparisons fed to TrueSkill to rank propositions, apply expert revision, and select top propositions to construct summary concept maps.
datasets: DIP corpus (selected 30 topics from Habernal et al., 2016)
baselines: A simple concept-map baseline: NP extraction, concept merging (stemming), relation candidate extraction from tokens between co-occurring concepts, relation selection, concept importance ranking using a Random Forest classifier, and heuristic subgraph selection to 25 concepts
limitations: Content selection is a major challenge: only ~17% of gold concepts appear among the top 25 selected by the baseline.
Relation extraction is a bottleneck: strict proposition matching scores are low indicating poor relation extraction performance.
Large and heterogeneous document clusters increase difficulty compared to prior datasets.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methods Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with a decade of experience building, evaluating, and deploying large-scale summarization and information-extraction systems in both academia and industry. Regular contributor to shared tasks and reproducibility efforts.
traits: checks whether experimental design enables reproducible results (data splits, seeds, code, runtime details)
requires clear baselines, ablation studies, and justification for pipeline design choices
evaluates statistical rigor of claims (agreement measures, significance testing, error analysis)
looks for robustness and sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of Open IE errors, annotator variability)
values precise description of crowdsourcing protocols and quality-control measures
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and pipeline are described and validated sufficiently to support the claims and to be reliably reproduced by others.

reviewer_b.name: Summarization & Knowledge Representation Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor working on multidocument summarization, knowledge graph/ontology induction, and evaluation methodologies; experienced in designing corpora and evaluation protocols to influence downstream research directions.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior work on summarization, concept-map generation, and crowdsourced evaluation
values dataset quality, coverage, and long-term usefulness to the community
examines how well the proposed resource and method address real-world needs and research gaps
looks for convincing qualitative analyses and demonstrations of impact on downstream tasks or evaluation practice
considers scalability and applicability across domains and languages
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Examines the manuscript for clear contribution to the field, significance of the released corpus and protocol, and whether claims about usefulness and novelty are substantiated both empirically and qualitatively.
